statcast.definition: MLB's ball-tracking system, installed in every stadium, that uses radar and high-speed cameras to record pitch velocity, exit velocity, launch angle, player positioning, and other on-field measurements. Deployed league-wide in 2015.

baseball savant.definition: The public MLB website (baseballsavant.mlb.com) where Statcast data is published for every player and game. Each pitcher page includes the Movement Profile chart, percentile rankings, pitch-by-pitch data, and advanced metrics like Stuff+ and Location+. Each hitter page includes exit velocity, launch angle, barrel rate, and batted-ball distributions. Baseball Savant is the primary source for the Statcast-era statistics discussed throughout these articles.

movement profile.definition: A scatter plot on Baseball Savant's pitcher pages that charts each pitch type by its horizontal and vertical movement, measured in inches relative to a theoretical spinless ball. The X-axis shows arm-side or glove-side break; the Y-axis shows how much the pitch rises or drops due to spin, independent of gravity. The shape of the chart — whether it's tall, wide, or clustered near center — reveals the geometric design of a pitcher's arsenal and predicts strikeout tendency, ground ball rate, and the type of contact the pitcher is likely to generate.

exit velocity.definition: The speed at which the ball leaves the bat after contact, measured in miles per hour. Higher exit velocity indicates harder contact. League average is approximately 88 to 89 mph; among the best hitters in baseball, average exit velocity typically falls between 92 and 95 mph or above.

launch angle.definition: The vertical angle at which the ball leaves the bat. A launch angle of zero degrees produces a flat line drive; negative values produce ground balls; roughly 10 to 30 degrees produces the highest rates of home runs and extra-base hits; above 45 degrees produces a pop-up.

barrel.definition: A batted ball with an exit velocity of at least 98 mph and a launch angle between roughly 26 and 30 degrees. Balls meeting both thresholds are associated with very high slugging percentages and produce extra-base hits at a high rate.

barrel rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play that qualify as barrels, meeting Statcast thresholds for both exit velocity and launch angle. League average is around 6 to 8 percent; among elite hitters, barrel rate is typically 15 to 20 percent or higher.

barrel rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher that qualify as barrels — meeting Statcast thresholds for both exit velocity and launch angle. League average is around 6 to 8 percent. A pitcher who consistently suppresses barrel rate is limiting the most damaging contact possible. Barrel rate allowed is closely correlated with ERA and xERA and is one of the better indicators of how well a pitcher is actually pitching, independent of sequencing or defense.

attack angle.definition: The slope of the bat's path through the hitting zone at the moment of contact, measured in degrees. A flat swing approaches zero degrees; a steep uppercut reaches 15 to 20 degrees or more. Attack angle is a primary determinant of launch angle: a flatter path produces ground balls and line drives, while a more upward path produces fly balls.

xslg.definition: A Statcast metric estimating what a hitter's slugging percentage would have been based solely on the quality of contact, specifically exit velocity and launch angle, without regard to where the ball actually landed. League average xSLG is roughly .390 to .400; elite hitters typically exceed .500. Differences between xSLG and actual slugging percentage can indicate luck or park effects.

expected slugging.definition: A Statcast metric estimating what a hitter's slugging percentage would have been based solely on the quality of contact, specifically exit velocity and launch angle, without regard to where the ball actually landed. League average xSLG is roughly .390 to .400; elite hitters typically exceed .500. Differences between xSLG and actual slugging percentage can indicate luck or park effects.

bat speed.definition: The speed of the bat barrel at the moment of contact, measured in miles per hour. Higher bat speed generates greater power and allows hitters more time to read a pitch before committing to a swing. League average is approximately 70 to 71 mph; elite bat speed is generally 75 mph or above. Bat speed tends to decline gradually with age.

hard-hit rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play struck at 95 mph exit velocity or harder. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent; elite hitters typically reach 45 percent or above. Hard-hit rate measures the consistency of strong contact independent of outcome and is among the more stable contact quality metrics year to year.

hard-hit rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher struck at 95 mph exit velocity or harder. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent. A low hard-hit rate allowed indicates the pitcher is limiting damaging contact; a high rate is a warning sign even when ERA looks acceptable. Unlike batter hard-hit rate where higher is better, for pitchers lower is better.

ground ball rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play that are ground balls. League average is approximately 43 to 45 percent. Power hitters typically have lower ground ball rates, since home runs require the ball to be airborne; a rate above 50 percent tends to suppress power production significantly. An increase in ground ball rate often indicates a change in swing mechanics — typically a flatter or more downward swing path.

gb% (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play that are ground balls. League average is approximately 43 to 45 percent. Power hitters typically have lower ground ball rates, since home runs require the ball to be airborne; a rate above 50 percent tends to suppress power production significantly. An increase in ground ball rate often indicates a change in swing mechanics — typically a flatter or more downward swing path.

ground ball rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher that are ground balls. League average is approximately 43 to 45 percent. A high ground ball rate is generally favorable for pitchers: ground balls stay in the park, convert to outs at a high rate, and limit the damage when hits do occur. Pitchers who generate ground balls consistently — typically by tunneling pitches at the bottom of the zone — are more resistant to home run damage and tend to produce more stable ERAs than fly ball pitchers.

gb% (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher that are ground balls. League average is approximately 43 to 45 percent. A high ground ball rate is generally favorable for pitchers: ground balls stay in the park, convert to outs at a high rate, and limit the damage when hits do occur. Pitchers who generate ground balls consistently — typically by tunneling pitches at the bottom of the zone — are more resistant to home run damage and tend to produce more stable ERAs than fly ball pitchers.

fly ball rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play classified as fly balls. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent. Since home runs can only be produced on fly balls, hitters with high fly ball rates and strong exit velocity tend to generate more home run power. A drop in fly ball rate often explains a decline in home run output.

fb% (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's balls in play classified as fly balls. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent. Since home runs can only be produced on fly balls, hitters with high fly ball rates and strong exit velocity tend to generate more home run power. A drop in fly ball rate often explains a decline in home run output.

fly ball rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher that are fly balls. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent. A high fly ball rate increases home run vulnerability, since only fly balls can leave the park. Fly ball pitchers typically need to miss bats at a higher rate to compensate for the home run risk — a pitcher who allows fly balls but generates a lot of strikeouts can remain effective, while one who allows fly balls without missing bats is particularly susceptible to big innings.

fb% (pitcher).definition: The percentage of balls put in play against a pitcher that are fly balls. League average is approximately 35 to 38 percent. A high fly ball rate increases home run vulnerability, since only fly balls can leave the park. Fly ball pitchers typically need to miss bats at a higher rate to compensate for the home run risk — a pitcher who allows fly balls but generates a lot of strikeouts can remain effective, while one who allows fly balls without missing bats is particularly susceptible to big innings.

iso.definition: Slugging percentage minus batting average. By removing singles, ISO isolates a hitter's extra-base production. League average is approximately .140 to .150; a .200 ISO is considered strong; .250 or above is elite.

isolated power.definition: Slugging percentage minus batting average. By removing singles, ISO isolates a hitter's extra-base production. League average is approximately .140 to .150; a .200 ISO is considered strong; .250 or above is elite.

adp.definition: The average position at which a player is selected across a large sample of fantasy baseball drafts. ADP represents the consensus market valuation for a player heading into draft season.

slugging percentage.definition: Total bases divided by at-bats. Singles count as one base, doubles as two, triples as three, and home runs as four. Unlike batting average, slugging percentage weights extra-base hits more heavily than singles. The MLB average is typically near .400; a .500 slugging percentage is considered excellent.

batting average.definition: Hits divided by at-bats. League average is typically around .250; a .300 average is considered excellent. Batting average treats all hits equally and does not account for walks or the type of hit.

fantasy baseball.definition: A competition in which participants draft real MLB players and accumulate points based on those players' actual statistics during the season. Common scoring categories include home runs, batting average, RBIs, stolen bases, wins, saves, strikeouts, and ERA, though formats vary.

rbi.definition: A counting statistic credited to a batter when his plate appearance results in a run scoring. RBI totals are heavily influenced by lineup position and the number of baserunners who precede a hitter, making them as much a reflection of opportunity as individual skill.

rbis.definition: A counting statistic credited to a batter when his plate appearance results in a run scoring. RBI totals are heavily influenced by lineup position and the number of baserunners who precede a hitter, making them as much a reflection of opportunity as individual skill.

k% (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that end in a strikeout. League average is approximately 22 to 23 percent. A high strikeout rate reduces batting average and lowers fantasy value in traditional scoring formats. Elite contact hitters typically have strikeout rates below 15 percent; aggressive power hitters often exceed 25 to 30 percent. Hitters with steep attack angles often have elevated strikeout rates, since an upward swing path makes low pitches more difficult to contact.

strikeout rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that end in a strikeout. League average is approximately 22 to 23 percent. A high strikeout rate reduces batting average and lowers fantasy value in traditional scoring formats. Elite contact hitters typically have strikeout rates below 15 percent; aggressive power hitters often exceed 25 to 30 percent. Hitters with steep attack angles often have elevated strikeout rates, since an upward swing path makes low pitches more difficult to contact.

walk rate (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances resulting in a base on balls. Walk rate reflects plate discipline and the ability to recognize pitches outside the strike zone. League average is approximately 8 to 9 percent; a rate of 12 percent or above is considered excellent. It is among the most stable year-to-year offensive metrics and a reliable predictor of on-base percentage.

bb% (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances resulting in a base on balls. Walk rate reflects plate discipline and the ability to recognize pitches outside the strike zone. League average is approximately 8 to 9 percent; a rate of 12 percent or above is considered excellent. It is among the most stable year-to-year offensive metrics and a reliable predictor of on-base percentage.

walk rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of batters faced by a pitcher who draw a walk. Walk rate is the primary measure of a pitcher's command — the ability to throw strikes consistently without giving away free bases. League average is approximately 8 to 9 percent; a rate below 7 percent is considered strong; elite command pitchers sustain below 5 percent. A high walk rate compounds the damage from hits, since free baserunners lower the threshold for runs to score.

bb% (pitcher).definition: The percentage of batters faced by a pitcher who draw a walk. Walk rate is the primary measure of a pitcher's command — the ability to throw strikes consistently without giving away free bases. League average is approximately 8 to 9 percent; a rate below 7 percent is considered strong; elite command pitchers sustain below 5 percent. A high walk rate compounds the damage from hits, since free baserunners lower the threshold for runs to score.

box score.definition: The traditional statistical summary of a baseball game, listing each player's at-bats, hits, runs, RBIs, home runs, and other standard categories. Box scores record results but do not reflect the quality of contact behind them.

fast-swing rate.definition: The percentage of a batter's swings in which bat speed reaches 75 mph or above. League average is approximately 40 to 45 percent; elite power hitters typically exceed 55 percent. A high fast-swing rate indicates consistent power generation. Declining fast-swing rate is often an early indicator of age-related decline in bat speed, sometimes appearing in Statcast data before traditional statistics reflect it.

spring training.definition: The pre-season period, typically February through March, in which teams hold workouts and play exhibition games before Opening Day. Most spring statistics are considered unreliable due to uneven competition and the presence of minor league players. Batted-ball shape metrics such as ground ball rate are an exception, since they reflect swing mechanics rather than results and are not affected by the quality of opposing pitchers.

line drive.definition: A batted ball that travels sharply on a relatively flat trajectory, typically with a launch angle between 10 and 25 degrees. League average line drive rate is approximately 18 to 21 percent; strong contact hitters typically exceed 22 percent. Line drives produce the highest batting average of any batted ball type.

breaking ball.definition: A pitch that moves laterally or downward as it approaches the plate, as opposed to a fastball traveling in a straight line. Curveballs and sliders are the most common types. Breaking balls are thrown slower than fastballs and are designed to induce weak contact or swings and misses by changing direction late.

curveball.definition: A breaking ball with topspin that causes it to drop more than gravity alone would produce, giving it negative induced vertical break. Curveballs typically sit 72 to 80 mph, roughly 15 to 20 mph below a pitcher's fastball. The best curveballs generate 10 or more inches of downward break below the IVB baseline and produce high swing-and-miss rates by diving out of the strike zone after appearing hittable out of the hand.

slider.definition: A breaking ball that combines glove-side horizontal break with downward movement, typically thrown 82 to 88 mph — faster than a curveball, slower than a cutter. Sliders sit between those two pitches on the movement chart: more vertical than a sweeper, more tilted than a cutter. Late-breaking sliders generate swing-and-miss rates by leaving the strike zone after a hitter has committed to swinging.

four-seam fastball.definition: The most common pitch in baseball — a fastball thrown with backspin, gripped across the four seams, designed to travel in a straight line with maximum velocity and high induced vertical break. League average four-seam velocity is approximately 93 to 94 mph; elite velocity is 97 mph or above. The backspin reduces the effect of gravity, causing the ball to arrive higher in the zone than a hitter expects — the perceptual illusion commonly called a rising fastball.

pitch velocity.definition: The speed of a pitch as it leaves the pitcher's hand, measured in miles per hour at release. League average fastball velocity is approximately 93 to 94 mph; elite velocity is generally considered 97 mph or above. Pitch velocity is distinct from exit velocity, which measures the speed of the ball off the bat — a pitcher's velocity is what he throws, an exit velocity is what a hitter produces in response.

4-seam fastball.definition: The most common pitch in baseball — a fastball thrown with backspin, gripped across the four seams, designed to travel in a straight line with maximum velocity and high induced vertical break. League average four-seam velocity is approximately 93 to 94 mph; elite velocity is 97 mph or above. The backspin reduces the effect of gravity, causing the ball to arrive higher in the zone than a hitter expects — the perceptual illusion commonly called a rising fastball.

sinker.definition: A fastball with heavy arm-side run and reduced induced vertical break, causing it to arrive lower in the zone than a four-seam fastball thrown at the same speed. Sinkers are typically thrown at 91 to 95 mph and are designed to generate ground ball contact by diving beneath the barrel of the bat at the last moment. Pitchers who throw effective sinkers tend to produce high ground ball rates and low home run rates, since batters who square up a sinker tend to hit the top of the ball.

sweeper.definition: A breaking ball that prioritizes horizontal glove-side movement over vertical drop, typically sweeping 15 to 20 or more inches across the plate. Unlike a traditional slider, which breaks both down and away, the sweeper travels on a flatter, wider arc. Sweepers have become increasingly common since around 2021 and generate high swing-and-miss rates, particularly against same-handed batters, by breaking so far off the plate that any contact tends to be weak and off the end of the bat.

splinker.definition: An informal name for Paul Skenes's primary off-speed pitch — a split-finger fastball (FS) that sits at approximately 93 to 94 mph with arm-side run nearly identical to his four-seam fastball but almost zero induced vertical break, causing it to dive through the bottom of the zone. The name combines splitter and sinker. Its value comes from the pairing: the four-seamer and the splinker leave Skenes's hand on the same plane and arrive more than 11 inches apart vertically, forcing hitters to guess a height they cannot see coming.

pitch tunneling.definition: The practice of designing a pitch arsenal so that two or more pitches follow the same flight path through the decision zone — the point roughly 20 to 25 feet from the plate where a hitter must commit to swing or take. Pitches that share a tunnel point look identical until after the hitter has already decided, then diverge in velocity, break, or vertical height. Skenes's four-seam/splinker pairing is a textbook example: both pitches travel the same horizontal lane and separate more than 11 inches vertically only after the tunnel point, which is why his secondaries generate more whiffs than their raw movement numbers would predict on their own.

tunneling.definition: The practice of designing a pitch arsenal so that two or more pitches follow the same flight path through the decision zone — the point roughly 20 to 25 feet from the plate where a hitter must commit to swing or take. Pitches that share a tunnel point look identical until after the hitter has already decided, then diverge in velocity, break, or vertical height. Skenes's four-seam/splinker pairing is a textbook example: both pitches travel the same horizontal lane and separate more than 11 inches vertically only after the tunnel point, which is why his secondaries generate more whiffs than their raw movement numbers would predict on their own.

changeup.definition: An off-speed pitch designed to disrupt a batter's timing by arriving significantly slower than a fastball while mimicking its arm action and release. Changeups are typically thrown 8 to 12 mph below a pitcher's fastball velocity and often include arm-side run and vertical drop. The deception is mechanical, not based on velocity: a hitter reading fastball out of the hand and receiving an 84-mph changeup is effectively early on any swing attempt.

cutter.definition: A fastball variant with late, sharp glove-side break — faster than most breaking balls (typically 88 to 93 mph) but with less horizontal movement than a slider. The late break makes it difficult to barrel squarely, producing weak contact on the inner half against same-handed batters and running into the barrel against opposite-handed batters. Cutters tend to generate more contact than breaking balls but consistently produce the wrong contact — jammed grounders, broken bats, soft fly balls.

extra-base hit.definition: A hit on which the batter reaches second base or beyond, including doubles, triples, and home runs. Extra-base hits are the primary driver of slugging percentage and run production.

projection systems.definition: Statistical models, such as Steamer, ZiPS, and ATC, that use historical performance data to forecast a player's statistics for the upcoming season. Projection systems are the basis for most fantasy rankings and draft pricing. They tend to weight recent results heavily, which can cause players with large gaps between actual and expected statistics to be mispriced.

iffb% (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's fly balls that are pop-ups, landing in the infield rather than the outfield. Pop-ups result when the bat contacts the bottom of the ball at a very steep angle, producing a nearly vertical trajectory and almost always an automatic out. League average is around 10 to 12 percent; a rate above 15 percent is a concern; below 8 percent is strong. A high infield fly ball rate typically indicates a mechanical issue with swing path rather than bad luck.

iffb% (pitcher).definition: The percentage of fly balls induced by a pitcher that result in infield pop-ups. Pop-ups are automatic outs independent of defense. A high IFFB% allowed indicates the pitcher is generating weak, off-center contact. Unlike most rate stats, pitcher IFFB% is difficult to sustain: pitchers with unusually high pop-up rates tend to regress toward league average over time, which is why xFIP does not credit pitchers for it when estimating expected performance.

ops.definition: On-base percentage plus slugging percentage. OPS combines a hitter's ability to reach base with his ability to hit for extra bases into a single number. League average is approximately .710 to .720; an .800 OPS is considered solid; .900 or above is excellent.

woba.definition: A measure of offensive value per plate appearance that weights each outcome by its actual run value. Unlike OPS, wOBA accounts for the difference in value between a walk, a single, a double, and a home run. A .320 wOBA is roughly league average; .370 or above is excellent.

xwoba.definition: An estimate of what a hitter's wOBA would have been based on the quality of his contact, specifically exit velocity and launch angle, without regard to where batted balls actually landed. League average xwOBA is roughly .320; elite hitters typically exceed .370. A significant gap between xwOBA and actual wOBA can indicate that a hitter's results were affected by luck or sequencing. When xwOBA exceeds wOBA by a wide margin, it is often cited as evidence of a potential bounce-back.

xera.definition: An estimate of what a pitcher's ERA would have been based on the quality of contact allowed, specifically exit velocity and launch angle on balls put in play, without regard to sequencing or batted ball luck. League average xERA is typically around 4.00 to 4.20; below 3.50 is considered strong; below 3.00 is elite. A large gap between xERA and actual ERA often indicates that a pitcher's results were influenced by luck or defense.

k% (pitcher).definition: The percentage of batters faced by a pitcher who strike out. League average is approximately 22 to 23 percent; a rate above 27 percent is considered strong; elite strikeout pitchers often sustain 30 percent or above. Pitcher K% reflects a pitcher's ability to miss bats and is one of the most reliable indicators of future performance, since strikeouts are almost entirely within a pitcher's own control and are unaffected by defense or batted-ball luck.

strikeout rate (pitcher).definition: The percentage of batters faced by a pitcher who strike out. League average is approximately 22 to 23 percent; a rate above 27 percent is considered strong; elite strikeout pitchers often sustain 30 percent or above. Pitcher K% reflects a pitcher's ability to miss bats and is one of the most reliable indicators of future performance, since strikeouts are almost entirely within a pitcher's own control and are unaffected by defense or batted-ball luck.

whiff%.definition: The percentage of a pitcher's individual pitches that produce a swinging strike. Unlike strikeout rate, which is measured per plate appearance, whiff rate is measured per pitch thrown. League average is approximately 24 to 25 percent; elite swing-and-miss pitchers typically exceed 30 percent. Whiff rate is one of the most reliable indicators of a pitcher's ability to miss bats regardless of how counts or at-bats ultimately resolve.

magnus effect.definition: The force generated on a spinning object moving through air, named after physicist Heinrich Magnus. In baseball, spin causes the airflow around the ball to accelerate on one side and decelerate on the other, pushing the ball in the direction of the faster airflow. Backspin on a four-seam fastball pushes the ball upward, partially canceling gravity; topspin on a curveball accelerates the drop. The Magnus effect is the physical mechanism behind all spin-induced pitch movement, which is what the Movement Profile chart measures.

ivb.definition: The vertical movement of a pitch caused by spin, measured independently of gravity. IVB is calculated by comparing how far a pitch actually moves vertically to how far a theoretical spinless pitch traveling the same speed on the same initial trajectory would move. A four-seam fastball with high IVB does not literally rise; it falls less than expected, creating the appearance of rising. A curveball with negative IVB falls more than gravity alone would produce. League average IVB for a four-seam fastball is approximately 14 to 15 inches; elite fastballs exceed 18 to 20 inches.

induced vertical break.definition: The vertical movement of a pitch caused by spin, measured independently of gravity. IVB is calculated by comparing how far a pitch actually moves vertically to how far a theoretical spinless pitch traveling the same speed on the same initial trajectory would move. A four-seam fastball with high IVB does not literally rise; it falls less than expected, creating the appearance of rising. A curveball with negative IVB falls more than gravity alone would produce. League average IVB for a four-seam fastball is approximately 14 to 15 inches; elite fastballs exceed 18 to 20 inches.

babip (batter).definition: Batting average calculated only on balls put in play, excluding home runs and strikeouts. League average is around .300. For batters, BABIP is influenced by speed, line drive rate, and pull tendencies, but fluctuates significantly year to year. A sustained deviation above or below a hitter's career baseline carries more meaning than a single-season spike; a .380 BABIP in a small sample is more likely regression waiting to happen than evidence of a genuine skill change.

babip (pitcher).definition: Batting average on balls put in play against a pitcher, excluding home runs and strikeouts. League average is around .300. Unlike batter BABIP, pitcher BABIP is heavily shaped by the defense behind the pitcher and by where balls happen to land — factors largely outside a pitcher's control. Most pitchers' BABIP tends to regress toward .300 over time regardless of prior results. A pitcher with a high BABIP is often a candidate for ERA improvement the following season; one with a very low BABIP is often a candidate for regression.

wrc+.definition: A park- and league-adjusted measure of a hitter's total offensive production, scaled so that 100 is league average. A wRC+ of 120 means a hitter produced 20 percent more offense than an average player in the same conditions; 80 means 20 percent below average.

hr/fb (batter).definition: The percentage of a batter's fly balls that result in home runs. League average is around 13 percent; the best power hitters sustain 20 to 25 percent over a full season. HR/FB rate fluctuates considerably from year to year and is among the less stable metrics in a hitter's profile.

hr/fb (pitcher).definition: The percentage of fly balls allowed by a pitcher that result in home runs. League average is around 13 percent. Pitcher HR/FB rate is heavily influenced by park factors and luck and tends to regress toward the league average over time regardless of prior results. A pitcher with an unusually low HR/FB rate is likely to allow more home runs the following season — which is why xFIP replaces a pitcher's actual home run total with an expected value based on fly ball rate and the league average HR/FB rate.

era.definition: The average number of earned runs a pitcher allows per nine innings pitched. League average is typically around 4.00 to 4.20; below 3.50 is considered good; below 3.00 is elite. ERA is the most common traditional measure of pitching effectiveness, but it is sensitive to sequencing, defense quality, and luck on balls in play, which is why advanced metrics like xERA and xFIP are often used alongside it.

xfip.definition: A pitching metric that replaces a pitcher's actual home run total with an expected number based on his fly ball rate and the league average home run per fly ball rate. By removing home run variance, xFIP isolates what a pitcher most directly controls: strikeouts, walks, and fly ball rate. League average xFIP is typically around 4.00 to 4.20; below 3.50 is strong; below 3.00 is elite. xFIP is one of the better single-number predictors of future ERA.

siera.definition: A pitching metric that estimates ERA based on the outcomes a pitcher most directly controls: strikeout rate, walk rate, and ground ball rate. SIERA treats ground balls as more favorable than fly balls because ground balls cannot leave the park. League average is typically around 4.00 to 4.20. It is designed to be a better predictor of future performance than ERA and is less sensitive to sequencing luck than most ERA estimators.

stuff+.definition: A Baseball Savant rating that evaluates the quality of each pitch based on its physical characteristics — velocity, movement, and spin — compared to league average. A Stuff+ of 100 is average; above 100 means the pitch grades better than average; 120 or above is considered excellent. Stuff+ measures raw pitch quality only and does not account for command or sequencing.

location+.definition: A Baseball Savant rating that evaluates how well a pitcher locates each pitch within the strike zone, independent of the pitch's raw stuff. A Location+ of 100 is average; above 100 indicates above-average command. Location+ and Stuff+ are typically read together: a pitcher can succeed with below-average stuff and strong location, or struggle to command elite raw stuff effectively.

arm-side.definition: Directional terms describing horizontal pitch movement, always from the catcher's perspective. For a right-handed pitcher, arm-side is to the right on the Movement Profile chart — the direction the ball moves toward a right-handed batter's hands. Glove-side is to the left — the direction the ball breaks away from a right-handed batter. For a left-handed pitcher, the directions are reversed.

glove-side.definition: Directional terms describing horizontal pitch movement, always from the catcher's perspective. For a right-handed pitcher, arm-side is to the right on the Movement Profile chart — the direction the ball moves toward a right-handed batter's hands. Glove-side is to the left — the direction the ball breaks away from a right-handed batter. For a left-handed pitcher, the directions are reversed.

run.definition: Arm-side horizontal movement on a pitch — the ball drifting toward the pitcher's throwing-hand side as it approaches the plate. A pitch that 'runs' moves in toward a same-handed batter's hands and away from an opposite-handed batter. Sinkers and two-seamers are the pitches most associated with heavy run; a fastball described as having 'late run' moves arm-side near the end of its path, making it difficult to barrel on the inner half.

cut.definition: Glove-side horizontal movement on a pitch — the ball moving away from the pitcher's throwing-hand side as it approaches the plate. A pitch that 'cuts' breaks toward an opposite-handed batter's hands and away from a same-handed batter. Cutters are named for this movement, but any pitch can cut: a fastball with unexpected glove-side break is often described as 'cutting.' Late cut is particularly difficult to handle because the pitch looks like a fastball until the last moment.

inning.definition: One unit of play in a baseball game. Each team bats until it records three outs — that's one half-inning. A full inning covers both halves. Standard games are nine innings; ties extend into extra innings.

count.definition: The current tally of balls and strikes in a plate appearance, stated as balls-strikes (e.g., 2-1 is two balls and one strike). A 3-0 count heavily favors the hitter; 0-2 favors the pitcher. A full count — 3-2 — means the next pitch almost always ends the at-bat.

at-bat.definition: A batter's turn at the plate that results in a hit, out, or fielding error. Walks, hit-by-pitches, and sacrifice flies do not count as at-bats. Batting average is calculated using at-bats as the denominator, which is why it doesn't account for walks.

ab.definition: A batter's turn at the plate that results in a hit, out, or fielding error. Walks, hit-by-pitches, and sacrifice flies do not count as at-bats. Batting average is calculated using at-bats as the denominator, which is why it doesn't account for walks.

plate appearance.definition: Any completed turn at the plate, regardless of outcome — including walks, hit-by-pitches, and sacrifice flies. Plate appearances are the correct denominator for rate stats like walk rate and strikeout rate. Every at-bat is a plate appearance, but not every plate appearance is an at-bat.

pa.definition: Any completed turn at the plate, regardless of outcome — including walks, hit-by-pitches, and sacrifice flies. Plate appearances are the correct denominator for rate stats like walk rate and strikeout rate. Every at-bat is a plate appearance, but not every plate appearance is an at-bat.

hit.definition: A batted ball that allows the batter to reach base safely without a fielding error or a fielder's choice. Singles, doubles, triples, and home runs are the four types. Hits are the numerator in batting average.

single.definition: A hit on which the batter reaches first base. The most common hit type. Singles count as one base in slugging percentage calculations.

double.definition: A hit on which the batter reaches second base. Doubles count as two bases in slugging percentage and are a major component of extra-base hit totals.

triple.definition: A hit on which the batter reaches third base — the rarest hit type. Triples require both well-struck contact and speed. They count as three bases in slugging percentage.

home run.definition: A ball hit over the outfield fence in fair territory, scoring the batter and any baserunners. The most valuable individual offensive event in baseball. League average home run rate is roughly 3 to 4 percent of plate appearances; elite power hitters sustain 6 percent or above.

hr.definition: A ball hit over the outfield fence in fair territory, scoring the batter and any baserunners. The most valuable individual offensive event in baseball. League average home run rate is roughly 3 to 4 percent of plate appearances; elite power hitters sustain 6 percent or above.

walk.definition: Four pitches called balls in a single plate appearance — the batter is awarded first base. Walks count toward on-base percentage and wOBA but not batting average. A high walk rate is one of the most stable and predictive offensive skills year to year.

base on balls.definition: Four pitches called balls in a single plate appearance — the batter is awarded first base. Walks count toward on-base percentage and wOBA but not batting average. A high walk rate is one of the most stable and predictive offensive skills year to year.

bb.definition: Four pitches called balls in a single plate appearance — the batter is awarded first base. Walks count toward on-base percentage and wOBA but not batting average. A high walk rate is one of the most stable and predictive offensive skills year to year.

strikeout.definition: A plate appearance ending with three strikes. A batter can strike out looking (a called third strike) or swinging. Strikeouts are the only at-bat outcome that guarantees the ball never leaves the catcher's glove — no chance for a hit, a walk, or a fielding error to change the play.

k.definition: A plate appearance ending with three strikes. A batter can strike out looking (a called third strike) or swinging. Strikeouts are the only at-bat outcome that guarantees the ball never leaves the catcher's glove — no chance for a hit, a walk, or a fielding error to change the play.

error.definition: A fielding mistake — a misplay that would have resulted in an out with ordinary effort — that allows a batter or baserunner to advance. Errors do not count as hits for the batter and do not count as earned runs against the pitcher, which is why ERA and runs allowed can differ.

hit by pitch.definition: A batter struck by a pitched ball who did not swing. The batter is awarded first base. HBP counts toward on-base percentage but not batting average. Getting hit by pitches consistently is a minor skill — some hitters crowd the plate deliberately.

hbp.definition: A batter struck by a pitched ball who did not swing. The batter is awarded first base. HBP counts toward on-base percentage but not batting average. Getting hit by pitches consistently is a minor skill — some hitters crowd the plate deliberately.

stolen base.definition: A baserunner advancing to the next base during a pitch, without the help of a hit or error. A stolen base requires both speed and good reads on the pitcher's delivery. Stolen bases are a standard fantasy scoring category and add direct value to runs scored.

sb.definition: A baserunner advancing to the next base during a pitch, without the help of a hit or error. A stolen base requires both speed and good reads on the pitcher's delivery. Stolen bases are a standard fantasy scoring category and add direct value to runs scored.

double play.definition: Two outs recorded on a single play. The most common type — the 6-4-3 — goes shortstop to second baseman to first baseman on a ground ball. Pitchers who generate ground balls produce more double plays, which is one reason high ground ball rates are associated with lower ERAs.

sacrifice fly.definition: A fly ball that is caught for an out but is deep enough to allow a baserunner to tag up and score from third. Sacrifice flies count as RBIs for the batter and do not count as at-bats, so they do not lower batting average.

sac fly.definition: A fly ball that is caught for an out but is deep enough to allow a baserunner to tag up and score from third. Sacrifice flies count as RBIs for the batter and do not count as at-bats, so they do not lower batting average.

balk.definition: An illegal motion by the pitcher while in contact with the rubber, designed to deceive baserunners. When a balk is called, all runners advance one base. The rules governing balks are numerous and occasionally arcane — the core principle is that once a pitcher begins a motion toward home, he must deliver the pitch.

save.definition: A statistic credited to a relief pitcher who finishes a game for the winning team, typically by entering with a lead of three runs or fewer and holding it. Saves are the primary fantasy scoring category for closers. The save rule has known flaws — it can credit a pitcher for a low-leverage hold while ignoring a high-leverage middle-inning appearance — but it remains the standard.

sv.definition: A statistic credited to a relief pitcher who finishes a game for the winning team, typically by entering with a lead of three runs or fewer and holding it. Saves are the primary fantasy scoring category for closers. The save rule has known flaws — it can credit a pitcher for a low-leverage hold while ignoring a high-leverage middle-inning appearance — but it remains the standard.

win.definition: A statistic credited to the pitcher of record when his team takes a lead it holds for the rest of the game. Wins depend heavily on run support and bullpen performance — factors outside a pitcher's control. Most advanced analysis treats wins as unreliable indicators of pitching quality; ERA, xFIP, and strikeout rate are better measures.

on-base percentage.definition: The fraction of plate appearances in which a batter reaches base by any means — hit, walk, or hit by pitch. League average is typically around .320; .350 is solid; .400 is excellent. OBP is more predictive of run scoring than batting average because it counts walks as the productive outcomes they are.

obp.definition: The fraction of plate appearances in which a batter reaches base by any means — hit, walk, or hit by pitch. League average is typically around .320; .350 is solid; .400 is excellent. OBP is more predictive of run scoring than batting average because it counts walks as the productive outcomes they are.

whip.definition: The average number of baserunners a pitcher allows per inning pitched, counting only walks and hits. League average is approximately 1.25 to 1.30; below 1.10 is strong; below 1.00 is elite. WHIP is a standard fantasy pitching category and a quick read on how often a pitcher puts runners on base.

quality start.definition: A starting pitcher appearance of at least six innings allowing three or fewer earned runs. A quality start equates to a 4.50 ERA for that game — average, not excellent, despite the name. It's commonly used as a proxy for starter consistency in fantasy formats that track it.

walk-off.definition: A game-ending hit, walk, or play by the home team in the bottom of the final inning. A walk-off forces the opposing team to literally walk off the field. Walk-off home runs are the most dramatic ending in baseball.

starting pitcher.definition: The pitcher who opens the game and is expected to pitch multiple innings, typically five or more. Most teams cycle through a five-man rotation of starters on a fixed schedule. In fantasy baseball, starting pitchers are the primary source of innings pitched, strikeouts, wins, and ERA contributions.

sp.definition: The pitcher who opens the game and is expected to pitch multiple innings, typically five or more. Most teams cycle through a five-man rotation of starters on a fixed schedule. In fantasy baseball, starting pitchers are the primary source of innings pitched, strikeouts, wins, and ERA contributions.

relief pitcher.definition: Any pitcher who enters the game after the starter. Relievers typically pitch one or two innings per appearance and are assigned specialized roles: middle relievers, setup men, and closers. In fantasy, relief pitchers contribute saves, holds, strikeouts, and ERA — but accrue far fewer innings than starters.

reliever.definition: Any pitcher who enters the game after the starter. Relievers typically pitch one or two innings per appearance and are assigned specialized roles: middle relievers, setup men, and closers. In fantasy, relief pitchers contribute saves, holds, strikeouts, and ERA — but accrue far fewer innings than starters.

rp.definition: Any pitcher who enters the game after the starter. Relievers typically pitch one or two innings per appearance and are assigned specialized roles: middle relievers, setup men, and closers. In fantasy, relief pitchers contribute saves, holds, strikeouts, and ERA — but accrue far fewer innings than starters.

closer.definition: A relief pitcher who specializes in recording the final three outs of a game when the team holds a lead, typically in the ninth inning. Closers are the primary source of saves in fantasy baseball. The best closers combine high strikeout rates with reliable command in high-leverage situations.

bullpen.definition: The group of relief pitchers on a roster; also the physical area — usually beyond the outfield fence — where pitchers warm up before entering a game. A deep bullpen gives a manager flexibility to protect leads and cover for a struggling starter.

rotation.definition: The group of starting pitchers — typically five — who take turns starting games on a fixed schedule. Each pitcher starts roughly every fifth day. The top of a team's rotation produces the most consistent starts, innings, and strikeout volume for fantasy purposes.

designated hitter.definition: A player who bats in place of the pitcher without taking a defensive position. The DH rule has applied to all MLB teams since 2022. Designated hitters are typically power-oriented bats whose value is entirely offensive.

dh.definition: A player who bats in place of the pitcher without taking a defensive position. The DH rule has applied to all MLB teams since 2022. Designated hitters are typically power-oriented bats whose value is entirely offensive.

southpaw.definition: A left-handed pitcher. A southpaw's fastball naturally runs toward a left-handed batter and away from a right-handed one — the mirror image of a right-hander's movement. Left-handed starters carry a roster premium because they are scarcer and create favorable matchups against left-handed lineups.

can of corn.definition: A routine, easy fly ball — hit high and softly enough that an outfielder can camp underneath it comfortably. One of baseball's oldest pieces of slang, dating to the 19th century. A can of corn is the opposite of a frozen rope.

frozen rope.definition: A hard line drive hit on a nearly flat trajectory with exceptional exit velocity. A frozen rope is ideal contact — sharp speed, minimal upward angle. The term is also used for a strong, flat throw from an outfielder to cut down a baserunner.

meatball.definition: A pitch served up in the heart of the strike zone — flat, slow enough, right down the middle. A meatball is a gift to the hitter. Pitchers throw them by accident; the term implies something easy to hit and hard to miss.

heater.definition: Slang for fastball, usually implying high velocity. 'He's bringing the heater' means a pitcher is throwing hard. Used interchangeably with heat, gas, and cheese. A 'high heater' refers specifically to a fastball thrown at the top of the zone.

cheese.definition: Slang for a well-executed fastball — one with velocity, movement, or both. 'Throwing cheese' means working the fastball effectively. 'High cheese' is a fastball at the top of the zone, where induced vertical break is most effective and hardest to get on top of.

high cheese.definition: A fastball thrown at the top of the strike zone or just above it. High-spin, high-IVB fastballs in the upper third generate swing-and-miss because hitters consistently swing under them. The pitch looks hittable out of the hand and rides above the barrel at contact.

dinger.definition: Slang for a home run. Synonyms in common use: tater, bomb, shot, big fly, gone, long ball, yard work. All mean the same thing: it left the building.

tater.definition: Slang for a home run. See also: dinger, bomb, big fly, gone. Etymology unclear; in use since at least the 1970s.

chin music.definition: A pitch thrown intentionally up and inside, near the batter's chin, to push him off the plate. Chin music is a message pitch — a reminder that the inside corner belongs to the pitcher. Throwing chin music is sometimes retaliatory and sometimes preemptive.

jam.definition: To pitch aggressively inside, forcing the batter to make contact near the handle rather than the barrel. A jammed hitter typically produces weak contact — broken bats, soft grounders to the pull side, foul tips. The word is also used situationally: a pitcher 'in a jam' has runners on base with few outs.

painting the black.definition: Throwing pitches precisely on the outer edges of the strike zone — the black border of home plate. A pitcher who paints the black hits the boundary of the zone consistently, generating called strikes on pitches that barely qualify. It's the high-command version of nibbling: accurate rather than safe.

rubber game.definition: The deciding game of a series when each team has won an equal number of games. In a three-game series, the third game is the rubber game. The winner takes the series; the loser goes home with a split at best.

no-hitter.definition: A complete game in which a pitcher (or multiple pitchers) allows no hits across all nine innings. Batters can still reach base via walks, errors, or hit by pitch — which distinguishes a no-hitter from a perfect game. No-hitters are rare enough to be news.

perfect game.definition: A complete game in which the pitcher retires all 27 batters faced — no hits, walks, errors, or baserunners of any kind. The rarest individual feat in baseball. Fewer than 25 have been thrown in all of MLB history.

walk-off.definition: A game-ending play by the home team in their final at-bat that gives them the lead — forcing the visiting team to walk off the field. Walk-off home runs are the most dramatic ending in baseball, but any hit, walk, or error that scores the winning run qualifies.
